sections.summary: Document title: 'Terrestrial Animal Health Code' (non-substantive title entry).

General provisions section lists edition history and contains a disclaimer that designations and presentation do not imply WOAH opinions on legal or development status of countries nor endorsement of specific companies or products.
Non-substantive copyright heading attributing the work to the World Organisation for Animal Health.

sections.section: All World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH) publications are protected by international copyright law. Extracts may be copied, reproduced, translated, adapted or published in journals, documents, books, electronic media and any other  medium  destined  for  the  public,  for  information,  educational  or  commercial  purposes,  provided  prior  written permission has been granted by the WOAH. The designations and denominations employed and the presentation of the  material  in  this publication  do  not  imply  the  expression  of  any  opinion  whatsoever  on  the  part  of  the  WOAH concerning the legal status of any country, territory, city or area or of its authorities, or concerning the delimitation of its frontiers and boundaries.
sections.summary: Copyright notice stating WOAH publications are protected by international copyright law, describing conditions for copying or reproducing extracts (prior written permission) and repeating the disclaimer about designations and presentation not implying WOAH opinions on legal status or boundaries.

sections.section: F O R E W O R D
sections.summary: Heading only: 'FOREWORD' appears as a document foreword heading with no substantive text in this excerpt.

States that the WOAH Terrestrial Animal Health Code provides science‑based standards to improve terrestrial animal health, welfare and veterinary public health and advises Veterinary Authorities to use those standards to establish measures for early detection, reporting, control or eradication of pathogens (including zoonoses) and to prevent their spread via trade while avoiding unjustified trade barriers, notes that absence of specific chapters does not preclude risk‑based measures, and indicates chapter adoption/revision years and online availability.

Explains that the Terrestrial Code includes a Glossary for key terms, that '(under study)' indicates provisions not yet adopted, and summarizes the purpose of Sections 1–7 (diagnosis/notification; import risk analysis; veterinary services standards; disease prevention/control; sanitary measures for trade; preventive measures in production; animal welfare) and Sections 8–16 (disease‑specific prevention measures), noting model certificates and guidance for importing/exporting authorities.

Summarizes specific issues addressed across the Code including compulsory notification of listed/emerging diseases, recommended use of specified diagnostic tests and vaccines per the Terrestrial Manual, principles for declaring freedom from disease, zoning and compartmentalisation for control and trade, standards for collection/processing of semen and embryos (including micromanipulation and cloning), bee disease control, poultry biosecurity and other targeted measures such as non‑human primate movement controls.

States that Chapter 1.1 describes Member Countries' notification obligations under the Office International des Epizooties statutes, making listed and emerging diseases compulsorily notifiable and encouraging reporting of other epidemiologically significant animal health events.

Recommends that specified diagnostic tests and vaccines cited in Terrestrial Code chapters be used with reference to the WOAH Terrestrial Manual and requires experts in diagnostic and vaccine-production facilities to be fully familiar with the Manual's standards.

sections.section: 3) Freedom from a disease, infection or infestation
sections.summary: States that Article 1.4.6 gives general principles for declaring a country or zone free from a disease, infection or infestation when no disease-specific chapter provides requirements.

Describes prevention and control measures across chapters: 4.4–4.5 set standards for establishing zones and compartments for disease control and trade; 4.6–4.12 prescribe measures for collection and processing of semen and embryos (including micromanipulation and cloning) to mitigate animal health risks; 4.8 notes inclusion of non-listed diseases for Member information; 4.15 covers bee disease control and trade implications; and Chapters 6.5, 6.6, 6.13 and 6.14 provide poultry biosecurity and Salmonella on‑farm prevention recommendations.

States that international animal health trade measures should be based on WOAH standards but that a Member Country may permit imports under different conditions and must scientifically justify any more stringent measures.

Requires countries/zones seeking recognition of FMD eradication to provide a detailed history of FMD (including dates, sources, outbreak distribution, species affected, last case and strains) and a description of the eradication strategy and corrective actions; it also requires comprehensive disclosure on vaccination policy and practice (legislation, dates, vaccine types/strains, potency/formulation, species vaccinated, identification and fate of vaccinated animals, certification/records, compliance with Terrestrial Manual Chapter 3.1.8, and postvaccination monitoring where applicable).

sections.section: ii) Was vaccination ever used in the zone ? If so:
sections.summary: Heading prompting disclosure of whether vaccination was ever used in the zone; no substantive requirements in this line other than the question.

sections.section: 6. -Provide the date when the last vaccination was carried out;
sections.summary: Requires reporting the date when the last vaccination was carried out in the zone.
Requires specifying the type of vaccine that was used.
Requires identifying which species were vaccinated.
Requires describing how vaccinated animals were identified.

sections.section: 10. -What was the fate of those animals?
sections.summary: Requires stating the fate or disposition of the vaccinated animals.

sections.section: 12. -iii) In addition, if vaccination was applied during the past 24 months, provide a description and justification of the vaccination strategy and programme, including the following:
sections.summary: If vaccination occurred in the past 24 months, requires a detailed description and justification of the vaccination strategy and programme including vaccine strains, potency/formulation/purity and any matching, species vaccinated, identification methods, certification/recordkeeping, and evidence of compliance with Terrestrial Manual Chapter 3.1.8.

sections.section: 19. -iv) If vaccination continues to be used in the rest of the country, give details of the species vaccinated and on the postvaccination monitoring programme.
sections.summary: Requires details of species vaccinated elsewhere in the country and the postvaccination monitoring programme if vaccination continues outside the zone.

Provides comprehensive sampling guidance for surveillance and monitoring: sampling should be statistically based and representative with specified criteria (source, species, category, health status, selection method, type, size), sample‑size calculation principles accounting for expected prevalence, clustering and preference for exact methods at low prevalence, recommended sample sources (food‑producing animals, food, feed, environment), and sample‑type specifications (e.g., at least 5 g faeces for bovine/porcine and whole caeca for poultry).

Advises that sampling be conducted on a statistical basis to ensure representativeness and robustness and lists criteria to consider (sample source, animal species and category, health status, selection method, sample type and size).

Specifies that sample size must be sufficient to detect or estimate prevalence or trends in antimicrobial resistance, be representative and unbiased, account for expected prevalence, desired precision/confidence and clustering (adjusting calculations accordingly), prefer exact methods at low prevalence, and exclude samples from which bacteria were not isolated from prevalence calculations.

Requires Member Countries to assess their production systems and target sampling sources most relevant to risk—food‑producing animals, food products, feed and the environment—with resource allocation guided by production volume and prevalence of resistant bacteria.

sections.section: 4. Type of sample to be collected (Table 1)
sections.summary: Specifies minimum faecal sample amounts for isolation of resistant bacteria: at least 5 g from bovine and porcine samples and whole caeca from poultry.

sections.section: 4. Type of sample to be collected (Table 1) Faecal samples should be collected in amounts sufficient for isolation of the resistant bacteria of concern (at least 5 g from bovine and porcine and whole caeca from poultry ).
sections.summary: Specifies minimum faecal sample amounts for isolation of resistant bacteria: at least 5 g from bovine and porcine samples and whole caeca from poultry.

sections.section: Table 1. Examples of sampling sources, sample types and output
sections.summary: Provides Table 1 examples linking sampling sources (herd/flock, slaughterhouse, processing, retail, feed, environment) and sample types (faeces, caeca, carcass, food products) to surveillance outputs (prevalence and exposure data) and notes additional stratification needs (age, production type, antimicrobial use).

sections.section: Bacteria subjected to surveillance and monitoring
sections.summary: Identifies categories of bacteria for surveillance, emphasizing animal bacterial pathogens prioritized by countries and stating AMR surveillance objectives (detect emerging resistance, track susceptibility changes, support risk analysis, inform veterinarians, and enable epidemiology/trend analysis); notes AMR data come from diagnostic labs or active monitoring but accurate prevalence estimates require an active sampling programme.

Requires sampling of Salmonella from food-producing animals, animal-derived food products and relevant feed, recommending animal samples be taken from healthy animals at slaughter/abattoir and feed samples at the feed mill for consistency and harmonisation.
Permits sampling of commensal bacteria (E. coli and Enterococcus faecium/faecalis) from feed, food-producing animals, their environment and animal-origin products intended for human consumption.
Recommends preserving isolates at least until reporting is complete and preferably maintaining permanent bacterial strain collections to enable retrospective studies (Article 6.8.7).

States that surveillance programmes should include clinically important antimicrobials used in human and veterinary medicine (referring to the WOAH list) while acknowledging resource limits, and requires use of validated susceptibility testing methods per the Terrestrial Manual (Chapter 2.1.1) with results reported both qualitatively and quantitatively (MICs or inhibition zone diameters).

sections.section: 3. Recording, storage and interpretation of data
sections.summary: Advises careful database design and long-term storage of raw laboratory data, recommends quantitative recording of results (MIC distributions or inhibition zone diameters) in a national database, and specifies metadata to capture where possible (sampling programme/date, animal species/production type, sample type/purpose, test method, geographical origin, animal factors, antimicrobial exposure and bacterial isolation rate).
States the purpose is to describe an approach for monitoring quantities of antimicrobial agents used in food-producing animals.
Introductory heading indicating that definitions applicable to the Terrestrial Code follow.

Explains that collecting antimicrobial usage data is essential for AMR risk analysis, interpreting surveillance, tracking trends, evaluating mitigation efforts and veterinary prescribing, and for transparency, risk assessment and communication (to be read with Chapters 6.8 and 6.11).

sections.section: C H A P T E R   6 . 9 .
sections.summary: Chapter heading for Chapter 6.9 on monitoring quantities and usage patterns of antimicrobial agents used in food-producing animals (Article 6.9.1.).

sections.section: MONITORING OF THE QUANTITIES AND USAGE PATTERNS OF A N T I M I C R O B I A L A G E N T S U S E D I N F O O D - P R O D U C I N G A N I M A L S
sections.summary: Document heading repeating the title 'Monitoring of the quantities and usage patterns of antimicrobial agents used in food-producing animals' (non-substantive).
Article marker 'Article 6.9.1.' with no substantive text in this excerpt.

sections.section: 6.9.4 Development and standardisation of antimicrobial monitoring systems
sections.summary: Article heading for 6.9.4 on development and standardisation of antimicrobial monitoring systems (non-substantive in this excerpt).

Identifies basic data sources (customs, import/export, manufacturing, sales) and direct sources (regulatory registration authorities, wholesalers, retailers, pharmacists, veterinarians, feed stores/mills, pharmaceutical associations) for antimicrobial usage data and suggests requiring manufacturers to provide such information to regulators as part of antimicrobial registration.

sections.section: 2 Types and reporting formats of antimicrobial usage data
sections.summary: Requires at minimum reporting the annual mass (kg) of active antimicrobial ingredients used in food-producing animals and collection of supporting data (animal population by species/type and weights, dosage regimens and route of administration); specifies reporting formats (agent/class selection based on mechanisms and resistance data, international nomenclature, conversion of IUs to mass) and recommends breakdowns by species, route, type of use and, where possible, regional/local/herd/practice levels.

States that interpretation of antimicrobial usage data should consider factors in WOAH risk assessment guidelines, notably number/percentage of animals treated, treatment regimens, type of use and route of administration.

Provides guidance for responsible and prudent use of antimicrobial agents in veterinary medicine for treatment, control and prevention in food- and non-food-producing animals to protect animal and human health and minimise antimicrobial resistance as part of a One Health approach.

sections.section: Objectives of responsible and prudent use
sections.summary: Sets objectives for responsible/prudent veterinary antimicrobial use (preserve drug effectiveness and safety, maintain animal health, prevent/reduce transfer of resistant organisms among animals/humans/environment, and protect food safety regarding residues) and recommends measures to achieve these goals such as improved husbandry, hygiene, biosecurity, vaccination, access to laboratory testing and alternatives to antimicrobials.

sections.section: R E S P O N S I B L E   A N D   P R U D E N T   U S E   O F A N T I M I C R O B I A L   A G E N T S I N   V E T E R I N A R Y   M E D I C I N E
sections.summary: Document title heading 'Responsible and Prudent Use of Antimicrobial Agents in Veterinary Medicine' (non-substantive).
Article marker '6.10.3 Responsibilities of the Competent Authorities' with no substantive provisions included in this excerpt.

sections.section: 1 National Action Plan for Antimicrobial Resistance
sections.summary: Competent Authorities must design and implement the antimicrobial-resistance portion of their National Action Plan using a One Health approach aligned with WHO/FAO/WOAH/UNEP GAP, allocate budget and training/communication resources, conduct regular monitoring and evaluation, incorporate disease prevention, biosecurity and animal-health programmes to reduce antimicrobial use, and ensure integrated surveillance for antimicrobial use and resistance across animal, human, plant and environmental sectors in accordance with Chapters 1.4 and 6.8.

The Competent Authority is responsible for granting regulatory approval for veterinary medicinal products containing antimicrobials per the Terrestrial Code and must implement impartial registration procedures assessing quality, safety, efficacy and post‑marketing surveillance; importing countries lacking resources should assess import controls, verify exporter registration systems or seek technical cooperation and request exporting-country quality certificates, while approvals must be based on submitted data and accompanied by guidance on target species, route, dosage and withdrawal periods, and by risk–benefit evaluations for animals and humans, with VICH guidelines to be applied as appropriate.

Animal handlers must be experienced and competent, account for breed/sex/temperament/age and rearing history when handling livestock, avoid mixing animals likely to be hostile, design facilities to respect animals' personal space, and avoid sudden intrusion into animals' flight zones (which vary with tameness) to prevent panic, aggression or escape.

Loading and unloading facilities should be designed or modified to minimise distractions that cause animals to balk, using measures such as adjusting lighting to avoid reflections and dark entrances, installing solid sides or shields to hide moving people/equipment, curving passages to avoid dead ends, removing loose objects, and levelling or concealing uneven floors.

sections.section: C H A P T E R   7 . 2
sections.summary: Chapter heading only: Chapter 7.2 (Transport of animals by sea) — no substantive requirements provided in this entry.

sections.section: T R A N S P O R T   O F   A N I M A L S   B Y   S E A
sections.summary: Preamble stating that the recommendations in this chapter apply to live domesticated animals (cattle, buffaloes, deer, camelids, sheep, goats, pigs and equines) and may be applicable to other domesticated species.

sections.section: Preamble: these recommendations apply to the following live domesticated animals: cattle, buffaloes, deer, camelids, sheep, goats, pigs and equines. They may also be applicable to other domesticated animals.
sections.summary: Applicability statement: the recommendations apply to live domesticated animals including cattle, buffaloes, deer, camelids, sheep, goats, pigs and equines, and may apply to other domesticated animals.
Obligation: the time animals spend on a journey should be kept to the minimum.
Article reference only: Article 7.2.2. heading provided without substantive text.
